data.title: Similarweb: Elongated Sales Cycles A Worry Amid AI/SaaS Scare
data.description:
Similarweb (SMWB) reported a decent set of results but R&D intensity is rising as the environment has changed quite a lot since our last coverage. While the company is still offering its core SEO oriented data suite to help digital marketing, it's necessarily leaning more into AI now with its won tools to increase Gen AI visibility, but also with partnerships to connect their data as a tool to be used by agentic AI. The issue is that as search practices are shifting to include AI now, they have to invest to migrate their market share into the new era. Sales cycles are longer and we believe there is a bit of self cannibalisation happening and it reflects in AI-related revenues growing quickly but overall revenue missing expectations. The new paradigm is also taking a while to sell to more customers who are transitioning possibly between more intensive use of the older tools to the new tools focused on AI. While the company might be doing enough to withstand the changes in SaaS brought on by the proliferation of AI, the prolonged sales cycles are affecting earnings direction and we believe the stock is getting caught up in the AI-scare trade on top of earnings disappointments.

Results

One of the features of the AI-scare trade is that a lot of solidly performing companies are very sensitive to sell-offs right now. The 11% YoY growth is below guidance due to the fact that some LLM deals haven't crossed the finish line yet and were hoped to close within the previous quarter. There was maybe around $4 million in LLM deals that drove the shortfall from expectations in terms of the quarterly revenue figure.

AI-related revenues reached 11% of sales in the Q4 which we understand to include both the monetisation of gen AI results optimisation, the sort of new SEO that is for coming up in gen AI responses, but also other licensing revenues that are making Similarweb data available for AI to use and monetisation of the LLM-based insight generator that the company has developed. The revenue comes from data access fees and also variable consumption based tariffs.

In general, there have been some positives on the revenue side. Multi-year contracts are now 60% from 49% of ARR, and more ARR is coming from large customers reflecting a solid land and expand. For large customers net retention rate was higher than 100% but overall for the company it was 98% which reflects some challenges at the top of the funnel. This is the data for the FY.

While there are some effects coming from revenues that were more one off hitting the NRRs that should normalise, and the partnership with Manus may generate more recurring revenue as it uses SMWB data and accesses a new markets as new way to distribute the SMWB offering, the lower NRR and less than expected sales is reflecting some general issues. Our concern is that with SEO becoming less relevant and GEO, which is optimisation for Gen AI search results, need to be sold to customers, there is a bit of self cannibalisation and it takes a little bit of transition time for the sales to rematerialise. This is also reflected in the prudent guidance reflecting a 10% growth rate in 2026, and an even lower growth rate YoY in the next Q1 2026 results. Things are slowing down, and with AI-related revenues growing significantly faster it must be coming from the traditional businesses hence our feeling that there is some self cannibalisation.

Bottom Line

In terms of results, they are fine but profitability is lower and R&D intensity is going up to 25% of sales in the latest year, 24% in the latest quarter, and isn't going to be part of the fixed structure to get operating leverage over. There were sales headcount additions that haven't delivered so much, and therefore profits fell and the sales productivity meant limited growth compared to expectations. While they may be making the right choices to stay out of the way of the AI-scare thesis from a technology perspective, they are paying the price for the disruption by:

  • Needing to spend more on R&D
  • Self-cannibalising some of the business
  • And that transitioning customers into the GEO era has frictions and sales cycles have elongated.

They trade a lot cheaper on an EV/Revenue basis than Semrush (SEMR) at 0.59x against 3.38x. The models are changing the landscape is changing, but there is probably a relative angle. The issue is elongated sales cycles and getting caught up in the AI scare trade on account of the associated sales pressures due to the changes they are making in their market. It's a messy area, as a lot of SaaS is, and we're going to stay away.

data.title: LP Building Solutions: A Good House In A Rough Neighborhood
data.description:
Quality almost always wins out over time, but “over time” can stretch out painfully long for investors holding on to quality players in weak industries. Such is the case for LP Building Solutions (LPX) today, as a weak U.S. housing market has hammered the oriented strand board (or OSB) market and pressured volumes in its siding business as well. A weak housing market, weaker prices, and weaker margin leverage were drivers of my neutral stance on LPX when I last wrote about the shares, and the stock is down about 10% since then. Rivals like West Fraser (WFG) and Weyerhaeuser (WY) have fared about the same.

I’m guardedly bullish on the idea that housing has bottomed or is near a bottom. With the industry being relatively sane on capacity management, improvements in construction in 2026 should support a little upside in OSB pricing, but 2026 is likely a breakeven year for that business. Still, assuming some recovery in the coming years, I can argue that LPX is undervalued enough to at least be worth a look, particularly as these shares are likely to trade up anticipating stronger housing numbers later in 2026.

Better-Than-Expected Numbers In Q4, But Still a Tough Quarter

Assessing LP Building Solutions' quarter is a bit complicated by the fact that when profits drop to low levels, even small absolute beats/misses can look large on a percentage basis. To that end, LPX had a better-than-expected fourth quarter, but certainly not a thesis-changing quarter.

Revenue declined 17% in the fourth quarter, missing by around 4%. OSB sales plunged 49% on a relatively balanced mix of volume declines (down 26%) and lower realized prices (down 35%). Siding revenue rose 6% on a 2% volume decline and 8% higher pricing, and ExpertFinish remains a strong growth driver with stronger volumes and pricing.

Gross margin declined 880bp to 15% on weaker capacity utilization, weaker pricing, and cost inputs. Adjusted EBITDA fell 60%, beating by 32% (or about $12M), with margin falling to 8.8% (from 18.4% in the prior year). OSB was loss-making for the second straight quarter ($39M on $136M in revenue), while Siding EBITDA rose 33%, with margin up 540bp to 25.3%. Both OSB and siding did better than expected at the EBITDA level—by about $3M and $15M, respectively.

A Potentially Conservative Guide In A Still-Challenging Market

LP’s guidance for 2026 was soft relative to sell-side expectations but could prove to be conservative depending upon what happens with the U.S. housing market in 2026 (particularly the second half of the year).

Management guided to $430M in EBITDA (down about 1% yoy) against a Street expectation for around $490M. Both OSB and siding were guided lower: OSB by about $36M (breakeven vs. Street expectations for $36M) and siding by about $12M ($450M vs. $462M). While West Fraser didn’t offer as much numerical guidance, directionally their commentary was similar, with expectations for some modest improvement tied to housing.

Waiting For Housing To Rebound

New housing construction is really the key driver here, as it accounts for around 50% to 60% of OSB demand. U.S. housing starts have declined every year since 2021, and most analysts expect single-family housing to decline again in 2026, with expectations ranging from around a 4% decline to a 1% improvement. Multi-family should be stronger, but multi-family construction typically uses about 20%-25% less OSB per square foot of floor area, so it’s incrementally less impactful.

Single-family home sales were down more than 4% in January, and supply is still pretty high at 3.8 months. While up-to-date housing start information isn’t available yet, the trend has been weak here, too, and homebuilders have increasingly had to turn to discounts to move inventory—hardly encouraging for near-term newbuilds.

At this point OSB industry operating rates are in the low-to-mid-70%s and with OSB prices in the $230/msf range, even the efficient operators aren’t making money. Companies are usually relatively tight-lipped about their cash costs, but I believe the bulk of North American capacity has cash costs today north of $250/msf, with more efficient operators like West Fraser and LP probably closer to that $230/msf figure.

With most analysts looking for OSB prices to average out to around $250-$270/msf (though some estimates are at $290/msf-plus), I think breakeven to slight profitability is a good expectation for the top operators in the space in 2026, including LP. This should limit capacity additions, and indeed, there have been some recent capacity curtailments (300 MMSF from Arbec and 860 MMSF from West Fraser) that amount to around 4% of industry capacity.

Siding Continues To Drive Growth

LP’s Siding business has been its not-so-secret weapon for a while, and I expect that trend to continue. With a range of positive features including cost, appearance, durability, and ease of installation, LP’s SmartSide and ExpertFinish have driven share growth in engineered wood siding into the double digits, and I expect this to continue.

Unlike OSB, siding is driven more by repair/remodel (around 45% to 50% of the market) than newbuilds. While the repair/remodel market has had its challenges, same-store sales are slowly improving at Home Depot (HD) and Lowe’s (LOW), and leading indicators like the NAHB/Westlake Royal Remodeling Market Index and Leading Indicators of Remodeling Activity (or LIRA) are both positive.

I expect ongoing share gains in 2026, and I also continue to expect LP management to prioritize siding over OSB when there are decisions to be made regarding adding/converting capacity. I would like to see more efforts to expand availability in areas like the Southeast; LP’s greater focus on regions like the Upper Midwest has been a relative positive in recent quarters, but long-term growth is likely to be more skewed to the Southeast.

The Outlook

While old news now, I think it’s relevant to note that LP is undergoing a management transition, with Jason Ringblom taking over from Brad Southern. Southern led a transformation of this business that instilled significantly greater rigor around profitability, returns, and capacity expansion, and LP is now a much better company for it.

Those benefits show in the share price, which significantly outperformed peers during Southern’s full tenure. I see no reason today to believe that Ringblom will meaningfully alter the company’s strategy, but transitions always carry risk.

At this point I’m modeling relatively flat revenue and EBITDA for LP in 2026, with revenue up slightly (less than 1%) and EBITDA margin down about 25-35bp. I do expect housing starts to slowly improve (if nothing else, the comps get easier), and with prices where they are relative to likely cash cost curves, I expect relatively tighter supply to support OSB pricing at around $260 for the year.

I expect a stronger recovery in 2027 for OSB; while I don’t expect a surge in housing starts or demand in general, those capacity curtailments should support prices in the $275-$285 range, and that should drive meaningful incremental profitability for the OSB business. Furthermore, I expect a generally healthier and less dramatic trajectory for the siding business, though with sensitivity to improving remodel/repair activity as well as improving housing starts.

Long term, I expect around 5% revenue growth from LPX as the company continues to gain share in siding, see more OSB usage relative to plywood, and benefit from price normalization. Good years should see EBITDA margins comfortably in the 20%s, and management has done a good job of shoring up the trough profitability as well. While I am expecting lower free cash flow margins going forward than in the past, that’s largely down to not expecting a repeat of those “super-peak” years in 2020-2022.

While LPX shares don’t look cheap on discounted cash flow, some of that is due to high near-term capex spending, and I don’t put a lot of emphasis on DCF valuation here given the complexities and uncertainties of modeling more than a year or two out.

My preferred approach is a normalized EBITDA valuation. For this I use an 11.5x multiple on my full-cycle estimate of $650M, which gets me to $106/share. While that’s a higher multiple than I’ve used in the past, it’s reasonable given where we are in the cycle and LP’s improved full-cycle profitability.

The Bottom Line

If housing weakens further and the economy doesn’t improve as expected in 2026, there’s definitely risk here. It’s also a cyclical, hard-to-model company even in the best of times, and while the growth of the siding business makes it less cyclical and commoditized than in the past, it’s clearly not immune to cycles.

I think LP has remade itself into one of the most disciplined, returns-driven, rational players in the space, and I think that’s worth something. Given that sentiment is still pretty weak, I’m tempted by the contrarian angle here, as I’m confident that LP will come back at some point. As I said in the open, timing can be tricky, and investors considering this name have to be willing to accept the risk that the housing/construction recovery will take longer to play out.

data.title: Lazard Global Listed Infrastructure Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

Global equities rose in the fourth quarter as shifting artificial intelligence (AI) sentiment and uncertainty around global interest rate policies drove markets. The US Federal Reserve delivered three 25 basis points (bps) cuts before signalling a pause in December, lifting market sentiment despite unclear US inflation and labour trends. In Europe, the European Central Bank held rates steady and indicated its rate-cutting cycle had ended amid improving conditions. The Bank of England kept rates unchanged before delivering a December cut but suggested further reductions may slow given weak growth and mixed inflation signals.

Portfolio Review

In the fourth quarter, the Lazard Global Listed Infrastructure Portfolio rose 4.9% (net of fees) outperforming the 0.6% return of its benchmark, the MSCI World Core Infrastructure (USD Hedged) Index.

Global transport infrastructure owner Ferrovial (FER) (4.3%) continued to perform strongly during the quarter after positively surprising in its results, demonstrating pricing power across its US Express Lane portfolio while exhibiting low sensitivity to current macro volatility. A key positive throughout 2025 has been increased user uptake of promotions on the 407-ETR in Toronto, Canada. Combined with ongoing price increases in the base tariffs, we see this as a powerful value-generation mechanism for the 407-ETR. Furthermore, FER's US Managed Lanes have averaged 15% price growth for each period over the past eight reported quarters. With upwards of 40-years remaining on each of the five US Managed Lane concessions, we believe that the group's ability to continue to deliver strong performance is well founded. In November, the 407 ETR announced a new toll schedule effective in 2026, featuring significant price hikes during specific travel periods to manage congestion. This level of increase exceeded our expectations and the uplift to the value of FER with no other offsetting adjustments would be an approximate 7% increase to our value, all else being equal.

Italian gas utility Italgas (ITGGF) (4.8%) continued its strong run, upgrading its long-term guidance over the quarter. Progress on the integration of 2iReteGas has been impressive and increases management confidence about the level of synergies to be extracted. We believe management has built significant credibility in stepping up the Italgas legacy business and see little reason to doubt their ability to deliver the targeted improvements at 2iReteGas.

UK diversified utility National Grid (NGG) (8.3%) benefited from persistently high inflation in the United Kingdom during the quarter. The Competition and Markets Authority issued decisions on several appeals related to the UK water regulatory review, determining that the allowed return on regulatory equity should be more than 70 bps higher than the level set by Ofwat last year. We believe the UK continues to represent an attractive operating environment for efficient operators such as National Grid.

US mobile tower operators, American Tower (AMT) (4.6%) and Crown Castle (CCI) (4.7%), have faced recent underperformance due to a combination of sector-wide and company-specific factors. Higher interest rates have raised discount rates and funding costs for tower operators, while elevated customer churn has moderated new tower growth. Following an initial surge in 5G deployments, global carrier capital expenditure has slowed. AMT's results have also been affected by emerging market exposure, including foreign exchange volatility in Africa and its former India business, though the firm is shifting capital toward developed markets to reduce risk. CCI has been weighed down by a prolonged and public strategic review of its fiber business, activist investor pressure, and leadership changes. However, none of these issues are new developments for AMT or CCI and therefore do not currently warrant any changes to our investment theses. We continue to own AMT and CCI.

Outlook

Equity markets have continued to perform strongly throughout 2025, including listed infrastructure. Given the combination of volatile equity markets and our conservative approach, we view current market conditions with caution. We continue to see limited pockets of attractive value opportunities, particularly in Europe. Although inflation shows signs of moderating, we expect it will remain above most Central Bank target ranges for several years. We believe that high bursts of inflation have positive cash flow implications for toll roads, airports, railways and non-US utilities. In contrast, the implications of higher inflation for US utilities are likely negative.

We have long cautioned investors about the valuation of the US utility sector and we have been underweight this sector for some time. While we remain cautious, we are beginning to see specific stock opportunities within the sector, which we may pursue in the months ahead.

The scarce valuation opportunities have led to a relatively concentrated portfolio where we believe the risk/return trade-off is favorable, however, this brings a higher degree of stock-specific risk. In our opinion, the only way to generate returns that properly compensate for the risk taken is through highly selective stock-picking. We caution investors to expect increased volatility in the short to medium term. Value is emerging now and on a 5-year view and valuations look more attractive on a risk/return basis. We believe returns available in the strategy look relatively attractive at this time when compared to a passive investment in infrastructure indices, bonds or in broader equity markets. We believe the preferred infrastructure characteristics we seek for all our investments will continue to serve our investors well over the longer term.

data.title: Kaiser Aluminum: 2026E After Triple-Digit Annualized RoR (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Sometimes a company will surprise you with the sheer amount of return that it generates over a short period of time. You will not have expected it, but you'll sit there with an excellent short-term return and wonder what happened.

That's the case, right now, with the Kaiser Aluminum (KALU) investment I made back in December, about two months ago. You can read my article on the company here and how it's "not yet done" as a "BUY". I even added more. My position in the company is far older than that.

To say that the return it generated has been favorable would be a gross understatement.

I try to be fair both when the ideas I post generate returns and also when they do not. In this case, I bought close to $100/share, and it's up over 40%. On an annualized basis for two months' time, this is close to 650% RoR, which is obviously superb. It's a very good return for my portfolio, which so far in 2026 has seen mixed results - some wonderful, some not so much. This one is definitely by far the best so far for the time period that I've been in the relative size.

So what do we do?

What do we do with a 40%+ RoR in 2 months in a company that's exposed to volatile commodity swings? Obviously, what I'm going to do here is update my general estimates for the company. See what has caused the outperformance and where the company might be going from here. I already updated my PT in my last piece, and given the assumptions I worked with, I view it as unlikely that I'd upgrade the target higher. At the same time, we can't really know how high this stock could go, so I'll at the very least have to review my valuation model based on macro assumptions. As you can see above, it's close to a 35x outperformance of the SPY - and this warrants some kind of reaction.

As of today, I'm reviewing 4Q25 (which was out today), but I'm focusing on the pre-market share price.

Kaiser Aluminum - Seeing further upside in 2026E or seeing normalization

If you follow my work, you'll know that one of the things that differentiates me from other analysts and contributors here on Seeking Alpha is that I'm fairly agile and flexible with both my ratings and my movements, at least insofar as it goes for a conservative dividend investor. Combine this with the sheer scope of my investments and my watch list - several hundred companies around the globe - and you have situations where, in the space of 2-3 months, I go in, I rate "BUY", and I go out again, rotating and rating "HOLD". It can happen quickly, and sometimes it does.

KALU has been on my list for much longer and offers an alternative investment to aluminum giants like Norsk Hydro (NHYDY), which still is the lion's share of my exposure in the sector. But what I mean by saying what I say above is to not expect me to require a certain timeframe or to not react when the market gives me an opportunity, no matter how that opportunity may look to the untrained eye (in terms of valuation).

40% outperformance in 2 months comes to an average of about 0.9% gain per day using a 22-day market day monthly cycle. Unless the company is severely undervalued, it's not outside the realm of extreme possibility that what we are looking at here is an overreaction.

If we are looking at an overreaction, then the right choice for us is obvious, provided that the price has gone too far. The position will be rotated.

My last PT was $100/share. We're now looking at a trading price of $144/share, which exceeds by far both my PT and, in many ways, is also higher than I considered the fair value potential to go. On that basis alone, the choice to me is clear. The company hasn't changed its operations in 2 months. There have been no material changes to the underlying industry, which means that the only thing that could have happened is either something completely irrational with no basis in company fact or performance or something earnings-related.

And indeed, it's the 3Q25 and 4Q25 results that seem to have convinced investors here. The problem is, these results were already included in my last article (including the 4Q25, which were reported today), and I've already modeled for a significant increase in aluminum demand for aerospace as well as packaging. That the company beat estimates of $0.8/share quarterly by more than doubling it does not change my thesis. Neither does the company's raising of its yearly EBITDA targets. These trends are part of the reason I viewed the company as undervalued about 2 months back. Here I'm going to look a bit at both 3Q25 and 4Q25.

The growth projects that the company has almost finished in both Trentwood and other assets have never, in my view, been in fundamental danger of not being completed. Yet the market may have included this in its consideration as well. For 4Q25, the company saw record trends with over $300M in EBITDA, which is a more than 25% YoY growth, with significant expansion in EBITDA margins - also positive.

But you need to look at the underlying long-term market trends for aluminum, and this includes the 4Q25 results. In doing so, you will find that there's more weighing on the company's future potential than would be justified by this sort of share price - even for the company's latest results.

The end market summary shows us that shipments are actually down YoY in both Aerospace/HS and Packaging, as well as automotive. In terms of volume, only general engineering is up. That means that most of the upside here is actually pricing, confirmed by the company citing price/mix as one of its key factors for improvement.

While price/mix improvements are attractive, they are less desirable to me (usually) than organic growth improvements, especially when talking about these sorts of volatile end-markets. To confirm this, the company's outlook for its key markets, including automotive, is actually negative. While some markets are expecting top-line increases with higher revenue conversion, the automotive outlook for the coming year is expected to be poor. Even with improved outlooks for the other parts of the company, I don't necessarily see this as enough of a reason to change my PT.

While EBITDA margins for 4Q25 are excellent, and the company's leverage as a result of very solid EBITDA is down, I already assumed a mid-cycle 20-25% EBITDA as my bottom line target. It does not, in my view, justify a multiple this high or a $140+ share price.

Also, the general assumption that certain key markets will generate above-average revenue or sales is one that's, in my view, wrong. Aerospace is not an easy segment - that is why the company is forecasting a 10% decline in the segment. The lauded packaging segment is also expecting a 3% shipment decline, and this reflects broader trends in the industry.

And if you want to "hang your hat" on significant longer-term growth in automotive extrusions or the more "general engineering" segment, then I have some trends in general aluminum, also reflected by much larger Norsk Hydro, that should give you pause. Lower shipments in automotive as well as other extrusions seem to be trending down, and not even strategic foci that the company is doing (quite well, may I add) will change the trends of an end market.

I'm a close follower of commodities and metals - and aluminum in particular, because of my investment in Norsk Hydro, which is significant at this time. That means that I know the way that the industry views 2026-2028E like the back of my hand. And while I'm not naive enough to believe this forecast can't change, I would view it as very unlikely indeed for the market to support what the updated performance and share price translate to.

Power concerns are the main issue, weighing costs. While availability is better in the US than it is in Europe, nothing changes that aluminum requires significant energy, and KALU is not having an easy time securing power at a good price. That's why Norsk Hydro has a bit of an advantage - it generates its own electricity for smelting. The increased competition with other power-heavy applications, such as data centers, is driving a bottleneck of energy not only in the EU but in the US as well. Domestic users of aluminum have seen cost increases due to increasingly curtailed imports from Canada and other regions due to tariffs. KALU does quite a bit of recycling, but many markets are limiting exports of scrap, which could impact these markets.

Also, while we continue to have aluminum demand upside from the ESG transition, much of the legacy demand sources for aluminum are all but gone or heavily curtailed, such as the Chinese property market.

That means that the massive domestic capacity of aluminum is likely to go elsewhere, which will put pressure on the market as well as significantly reduce demand.

I won't claim that the company has the largest impact from this. Hydro's is much higher. But I believe a key reason why KALU is up, and even why the 4Q25 results are record-level, is the significant market backdrop we've seen - and other than that, I argue this is sentiment-driven. The latest results do not change my thesis.

Valuation for KALU - Upside is severely limited

One of the reasons I liked KALU as recently as December was the significant upside from sheer reversal that I could see. KALU was incredibly attractive at $60-$90/share because this represented 11-15x forward P/E. At $144/share, that multiple is up to over 23x. The longer-term upside, even accounting for a bit of a surge after positive 4Q, was high enough.

23x+ P/E for a company that's BB- rated, junk, with more than 55% long-term debt to capital, a market capitalization of less than $2.3B and a volatility that far outpaces most of the bigger players on the market.

The 4Q25/full-year performance has lost most of its relevance to me. Even in the case of further significant outperformance, I wouldn't put that to a higher PT than the one I gave in this and in other articles. Even with the increased dividend, I don't see a conservative upside to something like 20-25x P/E as likely. The aluminum markets are too volatile to expect a 20-30% annualized AEPS growth and consider it likely based on this. A fair value target between $120 and $130/share is the highest I would be willing to go here, and that's the valuation target. The PT is, as I mentioned, at $100/share or below. This, to my mind, already includes most positives that could happen here. A 40% increase in my PT is not happening.

I'm maintaining $100/share in my PT, citing fundamentals and volatile operating conditions. I don't change my FV. This means that KALU is now overvalued for what I believe it to be. It also means that I am not unlikely to rotate the position, or parts of it, going forward.

At current valuation, even an 18-19x forward P/E is unlikely to generate market-beating returns. To get there, we'd have to go above 21x, which would need to imply fair value levels of close to $190-$200/share, which would in turn require price targets of around $150-$160/share - I'm completely unwilling to go there.

Risks to the company are as follows.

Risks to My Thesis

Aside from forecast inaccuracy and failure for the company over time, I believe the company's main risk remains exposure to volatile end markets, which remain so in Q3 as well as 4Q 2025, despite some overall upside from tariffs. The company's dividend remains well-covered by net income.

It "eats" about $50M worth of cash each year, but the company generates well above this level in typical net income - at least since 2022. I view net income as a better indicator of dividend stability for this company, due to the volatile nature of the end markets here, which can lead you to believe that, because of a bad year, the company is more volatile than it actually is. Risks here are not dividends, therefore, but pricing losing its correlation to longer-term trends, which is what I am focusing on in this article.

The level of valuation in this company has now pushed other concerns aside, and I view KALU as uninvestable, close to 40% higher than 2 months ago. Obviously happy with my own RoR and ratings, however, I may take profit here, and depending on how things look after market opening today after very solid full-year results, I might do that today.

A clear risk to the path I'm taking is continued valuation increase from an expectation of further market growth. In such a scenario, my FV and PT might not be valid, at least for the short term, and by selling, I may be missing out on further growth. The estimate of this growth or how high it could go, however, is at that point no longer dependent on fundamentals.

Thesis

Kaiser Aluminum is a not-uninteresting aluminum company active in 4 appealing end markets, and a good price means that I am and have invested in the business. It has a decent yield, not a solid credit (but hopefully improving over the next few years), and difficult, volatile forecasts, which account for the main risk now that the company is trading well above any 15-18x P/E. I still view the good entry price for the company as being around $100/share for the PT, which the company is now far away from. The excellent results for 4Q25 and 2025 as a whole do not change my thesis and assumptions, because I have for a long time taken these into consideration when establishing my targets, and I believe these trends to be part of a larger aluminum bullish trend in the market. This makes the assumptions we have here too risky to use as a foundation for a higher price target as well as a higher fair value target.

I am, as of this article, reducing KALU to "HOLD", with a fair value target of $140/share at the highest.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 2 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 2/5 "stars" here, which is not enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this and a price target of $100/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "HOLD" rating as of February 2026.

data.title: BOTZ: Positioned For Relative Outperformance As AI Digests
data.description:
Thematically and conceptually, the Global X Robotics & Artificial Intelligence ETF (BOTZ) is set up to succeed when AI supply chain growth combines with automation in the physical economy, i.e., robotics in factories and industries. In other regimes, like the one we are in, growth in one theme compensates for normalization in the other. A look at the portfolio suggests a heavier industrial automation bias than AI ecosystem players. Today, the portfolio leans heavily into capital goods (allocation tables in the subsequent section).

In the recent past, BOTZ's returns have been pulled up by the AI exposure - it has done reasonably strongly, but has not materially outperformed the broader US markets that are more exposed to the AI theme overall. Going ahead, I expect that to reverse. US growth valuations already discount continued AI capex strength. Near-term earnings growth may be partly absorbed by multiple digestion. Global industrials may not accelerate meaningfully, but should hold up relatively better, leading to a period of relative outperformance (aided by a heavier industrials mix).

Another dimension in BOTZ that helps its cause ahead is its leadership bias. From a methodology perspective, this is a market-cap-weighted portfolio consisting of companies with at least 50% of revenues derived from robotics, AI applications, and related use cases. So, BOTZ is not a representative portfolio for global automation and robotics - it concentrates its bets on leaders. This augurs well in a regime where global PMI levels do not indicate an expansionary phase. This phase of stabilization in industrial activity will reward leaders over broader robotics plays (like ROBT).

BOTZ is a Buy based on the expectation of industrial robotics leadership continuing to do well, and even outperforming AI plays over the next few quarters. This relative Buy comes with moderate expectations, though. There are risks to the thesis - BOTZ's valuations sit around 31-32x forward earnings and remain exposed to cyclicality. A high beta also indicates a high-risk, high-return profile.

Portfolio Insights - Not a Broad Exposure

BOTZ is a concentrated bet on leaders - the top 10 holdings alone account for ~60% of the portfolio. This is not a broad robotics play that will necessarily benefit from a robotics wave across the globe. For instance, a Chinese robotics surge is unlikely to impact BOTZ much. Similarly, an automation surge that lifts AI application software, consumer robotics, defense automation, or the autonomous mobility and the EV ecosystem will not lead to huge gains in BOTZ. As evident from the industry and sector allocations, BOTZ is strongly placed to gain from industrial automation, with some gains from healthcare (again, not consumer-facing). That is not a negative, but it is important to understand where BOTZ's real thematic focus lies (which is industrial automation leadership) to construct a forward-looking thesis.

The Two Pillars

Since AI and robotics are the two core themes in BOTZ, I downloaded the full holdings from the ETF website and classified all the stocks into relevant buckets as per their core positioning. This may involve some overlap in classification, but should provide directional indications for this analysis.

Leaving 7-8% of the portfolio as "others", around 25% of the portfolio is invested in AI themes (of which ~8% in AI software plays). The overwhelming majority of investments is in industrial automation, that much is clear. The allocation here is ~60%, and could be debated as ~69% if we include healthcare and specialty robotics in this bucket.

The more material holdings can be classified into three buckets. The first visible pillar is AI compute, led by Nvidia. This segment remains sensitive to AI infrastructure spending. A second material cluster is seen around the Japanese industrial robotics theme. Names such as FANUC, Keyence, SMC, and Yaskawa dominate global robotics export, but remain sensitive to global manufacturing cycles. Then there are other industrial automation players like ABB, Daifuku, Intuitive Surgical, and Cognex - catering to operational efficiency use cases in electrification, warehouse logistics, and surgical robotics.

Broadly, BOTZ benefits more from relative strength in industrial activity than AI upcycles. Fundamentally, today's backdrop shows uneven industrial PMIs globally. In the US, the ISM Manufacturing PMI moved back above 50 in January (~52.6), marking the first expansion in over a year after a long contraction phase. In Japan, the S&P Global Manufacturing PMI rose to 51.5, with new orders and exports growing for the first time in many months. In the Eurozone, overall activity remains at 51.5, though manufacturing is still weaker than services. Industrial activity globally is looking definitely resilient, although we cannot say that this is a sustained and synchronized expansion phase in developed markets yet.

And that is fine. We are looking for resilience and a better story in global industrials, not the best. BOTZ invests in market leadership large caps, with very low exposure to early-cycle or under-owned stocks that deliver growth in broader industrial activity regimes. This means it is designed for such normalizing industrial activity phases, where large-cap leadership remains relevant for growth (in broader expansion phases, growth spills over to smaller players).

The other key pillar - AI - is still supported by an unusually strong AI capex cycle. This could remain secular or return to some cyclical trends with higher troughs. More importantly, I feel that cycle uplift is already well recognized and heavily discounted - the easier gains have already been made. As we will see in the subsequent section, this relative resilience in industrials is already showing up, while AI semis plays are consolidation (and AI software is undergoing a sell-off).

Performance and Forward Indications

BOTZ's total return chart shows two trends in the recent past. The first chart below shows how BOTZ has mostly stayed below broader US growth (proxied as SPY) in the past one year. The gap has closed only recently. This is because the past year was AI-driven, and there, SPY is overweight, compared to BOTZ's relatively higher industrial exposure. I expect that to change ahead (already gap-closing trends evident) as AI valuations stay supported, but digest earnings growth ahead, while industrial activity stays relatively resilient and benefits the large-cap leadership there.

The second chart shows why the markets are conducive for BOTZ from a different angle - industrial leadership versus broader robotics plays (like ROBT). BOTZ's outperformance there is likely to hold until we start seeing industrial recovery across developed markets consistently, and that translates to robotics and automation growth beyond large-cap players.

A look at the relative performance of Nvidia versus ABB and FANUC confirms early signs of my expectations. These three stocks are representative of AI and industrial exposure - together they account for over 30% of the portfolio. Among the 3, Nvidia has clearly been consolidating since October last year. On the other hand, ABB and FANUC have actually continued a steady rise through what has been a cooling period for semis. Semis remain well supported, but upside looks limited too (for some quarters as earnings catch up). On the other hand, industrial leadership should continue to steadily outperform.

  • Global rates strategies were the main drivers of the fund's weak relative performance during the quarter.
  • The fund maintained its overweight positions in investment-grade and high yield securities, where it selectively added risk through new issues and took advantage of concessions amid expectations of elevated supply in early 2026. During the quarter, the fund had a neutral position in duration (interest rate sensitivity).

Contributors

Detractors

Asset allocation was the main contributor to performance, mainly due to securitized carry and the overweight positions in investment grade and high yield securities. Credit security selection was additive, particularly in the communications, banking, and energy sectors.

Global rates strategies were the main detractor, particularly the global cross-market rates swaps strategy's long positioning in Korea, New Zealand, and Singapore versus the United States and Hong Kong in November. The global cross-market inflation strategy's long U.S. breakeven (the difference in yield between nominal U.S. Treasuries and Treasury inflation-protected securities with comparable maturities) positioning weighed on returns at the start of the quarter. Mortgage security selection detracted due to mortgage-backed security pool selection.

Further insight

Consumer and corporate balance sheets remain robust heading into 2026, providing support for credit. Jobs data softened during the quarter, though resilient consumer spending data helped to ease investor concerns. We expect mild disinflation, fiscal stimulus, and gains from artificial intelligence to broaden out to be tailwinds in the months ahead. The Federal Reserve vowed after recent interest rate cuts to "wait and see", though we believe it will have a somewhat dovish bias as the balance of risks shifts away from upside inflation risk toward downside labor risk. data.title: Northern Core Bond Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
For most of the final quarter of 2025, the macroeconomic landscape was shaped as much by what investors couldn't see as by what they could. The U.S. government shutdown—lasting a record 43 days—created a significant information vacuum just as markets sought clarity on growth, inflation, and policy trajectories. Despite the data fog, the global macro backdrop proved “good enough” for risk assets. Global growth held firm, inflation stayed sticky but avoided more disruptive outcomes, and most central banks leaned more accommodative than hawkish. In the U.S., elevated downside labor market risk kept U.S. Federal Reserve (“Fed”) easing in play, leading to rate cuts even as the economy expanded. However, policymaker dissents emerged as the Fed Funds rate approached the Federal Open Market Committee’s neutral estimate. International markets—particularly Japan and parts of Europe—offered broadly supportive signals, though political uncertainty occasionally weighed on European sentiment. Since mid-2024, most major developed market central banks have cut rates by 150-200 basis points (1.50%-2.00%) or more, meaning some could be close to the end of their easing cycle. The Bank of Japan remains on a separate trajectory with a gradual pace of rate hikes from a highly accommodative starting point, including two in 2025 and two more expected in 2026. Overall, the global tariff environment proved less disruptive than earlier fears. U.S. monthly tariff collections rose but remained well below levels implied by announced policies, while statutory rates edged lower as deals and exemptions took hold. Still, investors monitored persistent trade-related risks alongside other potential headwinds, including slower AI investment, labor market softening, bond market volatility tied to inflation or fiscal stress, and ongoing risk of geopolitical shocks.

Financial markets capped a strong 2025 with 4Q gains across both equities and fixed income. Many fixed income segments gained around 1%. The Treasury yield curve steepened as short-end rates dropped following multiple rate cuts in the quarter by the U.S. Federal Reserve. Credit spreads were mostly unchanged in both investment grade and high yield, ending near their lowest levels in 2025. Global equities added a little more than 3% as non-U.S. regions outperformed the U.S. in U.S. dollar terms (despite slight U.S. dollar strengthening). U.S. equity returns were positive (roughly 2.5%).

The U.S. Federal Reserve continued easing, delivering 25-basis point (0.25%) cuts at each of its two meetings. However, an increase in hawkish dissents and adjustments to the policy statement signaled a higher threshold for future reductions. Even so, ongoing easing contributed to further steepening of the Treasury curve, as front-end yields through five years moved lower. The 2-year yield ended the quarter at 3.47%, down 14 basis points (0.14%). In contrast, longer-term rates remained more resilient, with the 10-year yield up 2 basis points (0.02%) to 4.17%.

Investment-grade (IG) credit spreads widened by 3 basis points (0.03%) to 0.73%, while high-yield (HY) spreads tightened by 1 basis point (0.01%) to 2.66%. In terms of return, IG fixed income gained 1.1%, with high yield slightly outperforming at 1.3%. Periods of short-lived weakness followed by a strong recovery mirrored the pattern seen throughout most of the year. As a result, credit spreads enter 2026 below where they began 2025 across many quality buckets. Fundamentals continue to look healthy, but AI-related capex is helping to accelerate corporate debt issuance.

Explanation of Results

The Fund underperformed its benchmark by 4 basis points (0.04%) this quarter. The Fund's total return was 1.06% vs. its benchmark, the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index, which returned 1.10% for the quarter. The largest contributor to the Fund's performance was the securitized bond allocation (0.09%). The Mortgage-Backed Securities allocation contributed 0.06%, while Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities and Asset-Backed Securities contributed 0.03% together. The allocation to investment-grade corporate bonds also contributed to performance (0.01% added), particularly due to security selection. Government bond holdings were a slight detractor from overall fund performance. The Fund was neutral to duration throughout the quarter.

data.title: eToro: The Deep Value Play, And Why I Am Still Waiting To Buy It
data.description:
If you screen stocks purely on numbers, then eToro Group (ETOR) looks like a mistake. It reports extremely bloated IFRS revenues because of crypto accounting rules, making its EV/sales effectively zero. It trades at a P/E ratio that suggests it is a declining legacy financial institution and not a growth fintech, which it really is. However, if you take a closer look at the stock, you find something very rare. This company isn’t just a brokerage; it’s a social network where people bet with real money. eToro has 3.8 million funded accounts, a balance sheet with around $1.1 billion of net cash, and a really sticky factor providing it with a moat called the “copy trader” feature, which traditional brokerage peers like Interactive Brokers (IBKR) and Robinhood (HOOD) simply cannot replicate overnight. The market is currently punishing eToro for its strong link to retail trading mania and crypto volatility. The recent Q4 results gave the stock a much-needed short-term boost. My take is that eToro is a high-quality business trading at a near-junk or decline price. However, the fall may not be over yet. I won’t be buying the stock today; however, I will be ready for the inevitable crypto winter decline in retail trading volume and prepare to buy this stock at further declines over the next 3-6 months.

The business model - and Copy Trader

To understand why eToro is worth taking a look at, let’s dig into the details of its business models and, most importantly, what makes eToro different. Most brokers are ‘utilities,’ and you would ideally go there, trade, and leave. Moreover, today, the stickiness that you would have with any broker is very limited, and it would take you just a few clicks to move your money completely away from one broker to another. This may not be the case with eToro, which has distinguished itself and positioned itself at the intersection of social media and finance. The core differentiator for eToro is its social graph. On the platform, every user’s performance can be made public if they choose to do that. You can actually see who is buying what, who is beating the S&P 500, and what they are holding. And most importantly, it just takes you a couple of clicks to copy them and instantly replicate their portfolio, including making adjustments in your portfolio based on the new trades that the person places. This creates a powerful network effect. For an amateur trader, you can get access to managed money results without paying the traditional 2% or 20% that you would pay to a smart hedge fund manager. You just copy some of the best-performing traders and get access to their trades in your accounts proportionately. At the same time, for certain popular investors, there is value in not leaving the platform because eToro pays you. The top-tier investors on the platform earn management fees based on the number of people and dollar value of assets that copy them. This keeps them locked on the platform, and they would ideally not want to simply leave eToro because they would lose a potential income stream. This social network reduces the churn in clients. Hence, when the market tanks, an individual trader on Robinhood would often leave the platform. However, a copy trader on eToro might just switch who they are copying, and instead of copying a crypto bull, they may end up copying the top value investor now but are likely to continue staying on the platform.

The revenue engine and 2025 results

We must ignore the multi-billion-dollar headline revenue numbers that are reported everywhere because they are inflated by IFRS accounting rules that force the company to recognize the entire gross value of crypto assets traded on the platform as revenue. However, the most realistic number to track income for eToro would be its commissions. eToro earns money through three primary sources, including trading commissions, interest income, and eToro money and non-trading segments.

In 2025 , its net contributions are ~868M, and eToro earns the majority of this through spreads between the buy and the sell price. The spreads charged by the company are usually higher than the average and are ~1%, reflecting the quality of its underlying. Personally, I don’t like this revenue quality much because of two reasons. First, I believe that this excessive margin is someone else’s opportunity, and there is a potential that multiple investors can switch to other cheaper brokerages like IBKR eventually instead of paying such hefty spread commissions. Second, I believe the very wide spread also reflects the quality of the assets and the asset mix that trades on eToro, largely consisting of speculative assets like crypto, commodity futures, and other assets that have a higher fee compared to stocks and ETFs, which have a much narrower fee. As you can see from the chart below, eToro gets >50% of its trading revenue from speculative assets like crypto/commodities and currencies as of 2025. The share was higher in preceding years. In my opinion, this is bad news, as the trading revenues may contract if the mix changes significantly without high overall trading volume growth (e.g., to lower margin products like ETFs and equities).

Its second income stream is interest income of ~$217 million. Like all other brokers, eToro acts like a semi-bank. They earn spreads on the billions of dollars of client cash that is sitting on their platform. While this is a high-margin, sleep-well-at-night source of revenue. However, I’m not super optimistic about this stream in 2026 and 2027 because as the interest rates fall lower, the additional spread that eToro can charge to their retail customers reduces significantly.

The third source of money is eToro money and their non-trading revenue of ~$100 million. This includes forex fees, withdrawal fees, and their growing debit card and payment ecosystem. Once again, I am not very bullish on this revenue stream going forward. To give you a personal example, I have a brokerage account on eToro, and earlier, I used to deposit currency in my brokerage in my local currency, which automatically gets converted to US dollars (at a heavy 2% forex charge by eToro). But with time, I learned that this may be causing significant leakage from the fees that I am getting charged, and hence, I used a fintech platform to directly deposit dollars to my eToro account. If you lose 2% returns in every currency conversion, your net returns will significantly lag the markets.

The danger zone and why I am still not buying and waiting.

The reason I am still not buying is the ‘earnings quality’ and the fact that there might be pressure on almost every source of earnings going ahead due to macro-driven factors. Additionally, one of the biggest reasons why I would not go all-in on eToro today is that despite its efforts to diversify into other asset classes like stocks, options, and ETFs, eToro is still structurally very long on ‘retail sentiment.’ While many analysts are celebrating the Q4 results by saying that crypto share in Q4 '25 is just 24% vs. 50% in Q4 '24, my point is that speculative high-margin assets like crypto, commodities, and currencies still account for >50% of the asset mix. They ride completely on retail sentiment. I believe that a large part of the retail traders today have not seen a life-shattering market crisis in their entire trading histories. Often when Bitcoin bleeds, retail volume in not just crypto but other assets can completely evaporate. In a situation like this, eToro will be left with very little revenue but a very high fixed cost base, including marketing and R&D. In a scenario like this, eToro is likely to see continued pressure on its price and multiple. We are already seeing some signs of this in both eToro’s price over the last 60 days and the broader brokerage market. Different crypto coins are bleeding, and retail engagement is softening. However, if the stock shrinks substantially more over the next 2-3 months, that might be my entry point for me.

Valuation- the margin of safety is huge

So now let’s distinguish emotion from numbers and deep dive into the valuation. It goes without saying that the discrepancy between eToro and its peers is amazing. Post Q4 earnings, the stock is currently trading at a share price of ~$30-33, having a market cap of roughly $2.7 billion. Of this, they have around $1.1bn in cash and cash equivalents on the balance sheet with almost negligible financial debt, giving it an enterprise value of around $1.6 billion. Essentially, the market is valuing the entire operating business of this brokerage with around 3.8 million users, multiple global regulatory licenses, and proprietary tech with a sticky factor like ‘copy trader’ at just $1.6 billion. Doing a peer analysis, I see that the P/E ratio, EV by sales, and price-to-book for eToro are significantly below its peers like Robinhood, Interactive Brokers, and even crypto peers like Coinbase. The implication is that the market thinks eToro is 3x worse than Robinhood because its earnings quality is so bad.

To stress test these valuations, I’ll try to run some sensitivity analysis and see whether the company can survive a severe bear market. In my first scenario, I assume that there will be a severe crypto/retail winter where the speculative asset revenue drops around 40% from the current level. There is no stock and ETF growth, and the trading in this category stays flat, so the total core trading revenue declines by ~20%. And lastly, the interest income falls due to declining interest rates, and the interest income declines by around 20%. Even in this disaster scenario, eToro’s revenue would roughly drop to around $700-770M. Additionally, because much of their marketing spend is variable, they can hit pause on their performance marketing when users stop converting to cut costs. In this scenario, they’re likely to generate around $100-$120 million in net income on a conservative basis. At a $1.6 billion enterprise value, you’re paying just 11x the earnings even in this disaster case.

In my base case of flat growth and around $200 million of net income, you’re paying 8 times earnings for the business. This is certainly a deep value play, and I don’t believe this mispricing will last forever.

Key risks

I think there are multiple risks that eToro faces, and the first one of them is a regulatory risk. Unlike US brokers, which largely trade real shares, eToro’s European business relies heavily on Contracts for Differences, or CFDs, their derivatives that allow for leverage and track the underlying assets. Regulators usually hate them. There is a risk that if regulators, including the UKFCA or European ESMA ban crypto CFDs or cap leverage on these instruments, eToro's high margin spread revenue and margin revenue will vanish. This is an existential threat to their profitability that traditional US retailers like Robinhood do not face.

The second risk is a US launch failure. eToro has been trying to crack the US market and has frankly not been very successful. It is a drop in the ocean compared to whales like Fidelity and Schwab and even sharks like Robinhood. Their US offering is very limited, and they lack multiple essential products like ‘options’ that power some of these brokers like Robinhood. In case they continue burning cash in the US without gaining market share, it destroys shareholder value over the long term. Personally, I’m not very optimistic on this front because the US is already a very crowded market.

The third risk is that of reputation and excessive gamification today. eToro operates on a fine line between investing and gambling or gaming. It has a colorful interface, push notifications, and copy trading mechanisms, and these tactics, while driving stickiness and volume, can attract regulatory scrutiny, especially around the suitability of these products. Any major fine for predatory marketing could crush the stock price and trust.

The game plan

I am currently very bullish on eToro’s five-year future and believe that in the long run, some conversion between social media and finance is inevitable and that they are literally the leaders in this category. However, I am bearish for the next 3-6 months. My strategy is to wait. eToro stock has a very high beta of ~2, which means that it drops twice as fast as the market. I am not very optimistic about the overall market direction in the very short term of the next 3-6 months and especially of the industry of stockbroking. I am looking to start a small starter position in the stock if it drifts to under $25. At that price, you’re essentially buying the business for free option value on the next crypto cycle and can significantly benefit if the overall crypto cycle goes up again.

Hence, I currently rate the stock as a hold or a watch at this price level and will start making an entry in it if it reaches the mid-20s.

data.title: Touchstone Focused Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund Highlights

  • Concentrated, high conviction portfolio
  • Distinctive approach is centered on linking valuations with barriers to entry
  • Seeks to invest in businesses that are trading below what is believed to be its estimate of the companies' intrinsic value
  • Focuses on businesses that are believed to have a sustainable competitive advantage or a high barrier to entry in place
  • Strategy provides the opportunity to invest in companies of all market capitalizations

Market Recap

U.S. equities were higher for a third straight quarter to end the year. Despite the government shutdown and a spike in job cuts, underlying growth remained resilient amid the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) completing a third rate cut in December and corporate profits exceeding expectations. The best performing benchmark sectors were Health Care, Communication Services, and Materials. The worst-performing sectors for the benchmark were Real Estate, Utilities, and Consumer Staples.

Portfolio Review

The Touchstone Focused Fund (Class A Shares, Load Waived) underperformed its benchmark, the Russell 3000® Index, for the quarter ended December 31, 2025.

Within the Touchstone Focused Fund, the sectors where Fund holdings outperformed the most relative to the benchmark were Real Estate, Consumer Staples, and Materials. Sectors that lagged the most relative to the benchmark included Health Care, Information Technology, and Industrials. Stock selection detracted from performance for the quarter. Sector allocation contributed primarily due to the overweight in Communication Services and the zero weighting in Utilities.

The three holdings that contributed the most to performance included Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) (Communication Services sector), Applied Materials Inc. (AMAT) (Information Technology sector), and Coca-Cola FEMSA, S.A.B. de C.V. (KOF) (Consumer Staples sector).

Alphabet outperformed in the quarter as the market has gained confidence in the competitiveness of the company's AI products and services. The company's Gemini 3 AI models launched in the quarter and surpassed rivals such as OpenAI's GPT models on popular benchmarks. Additionally, remedies recommended by the judge in the antitrust lawsuit brought against the company by the

DOJ significantly lowered the odds of some dire outcomes feared by the market. Applied Materials' stock outperformed as shortages in supply of semiconductor chips, including memory, are raising the market's expectations of a material increase in orders for the firm's equipment. Coca-Cola FEMSA was a positive contributor to performance as third quarter earnings exceeded expectations. The South America region outperformed on both volume and price/mix. Mexico remained weak but showed sequential improvement despite a challenging macroeconomic environment. Additionally, management's efforts to streamline expenses drove sequential margin improvement.

The holdings that detracted the most from performance included Oracle Corp. (ORCL) (Information Technology sector), Meta Platforms Inc. (META) (Communication Services sector), and Microsoft Corp. (MSFT) (Information Technology sector).

Oracle's stock underperformed as concerns arose about the company's financial capacity to deliver on its large AI backlog. Admittedly, low external visibility into the nuances of large customer and supplier contracts can cause the stock to be volatile. However, we view Oracle as a well-run company and expect management to remain financially disciplined. At Meta, management significantly raised investments in the pursuit of AI capabilities, causing the stock to sell off as cash flows come under pressure. Microsoft's stock has been weak as investors grapple with the competitiveness of OpenAI's GPT models given Microsoft's reliance on OpenAI's technologies for its AI capabilities.

From a market capitalization perspective, the Fund ended the quarter with a 0.0% weight in smaller cap stocks (companies with a market cap below $2 billion). The benchmark weight for this segment is 1.5%. The Fund had an underweight in mid-cap businesses, which now comprise 3.7% of assets, compared to a benchmark weight of 5.8%. Lastly, the Fund is overweight in larger cap businesses (companies with a market cap above $10billion). The weight in that segment is currently 94.4%, which is higher than the benchmark weight of 92.7%. This allocation decision had a positive impact to performance during the quarter. Cash holdings ended the quarter at 1.9%.

Investments made in international companies, which comprised around 4% of assets at the end of the period, outperformed the benchmark.

There were no additions or removals to the Fund's portfolio during the course of the quarter.

As the quarter came to a close, the Fund had an overweight in the Communication Services, and Consumer Staples sectors and an underweight in the Industrials, Materials, Consumer Discretionary, Energy, Information Technology, Real Estate, and Financials sectors. The weight in the Health Care sector was roughly in line with that of the benchmark. The Fund held no positions in the Utilities sector.

Outlook and Conclusion

With an additional 50 basis points of Fed rate cuts easing financial conditions and market fundamentals remaining healthy, the Russell 3000 reached new highs again in fourth quarter, posting a 2.4% gain for the quarter.

The U.S. economy has remained resilient in recent quarters. The primary drivers of growth have been resilient personal consumption, supported by higher income households, alongside continued massive investment in artificial intelligence infrastructure. Following stronger-than-expected 4.3% GDP growth in the third quarter, the economy is projected to see a modest slowdown in fourth quarter, largely due to a temporary drag from the U.S. government shutdown, with growth normalizing in first quarter 2026. Investors are also looking to tax policy as a potential tailwind, as accelerated depreciation should boost capital expenditures and individual tax cuts should support consumer spending.

Earnings beats and positive revisions supported the market in the quarter, with S&P 500 earnings increasing 13.5% year-over-year in the third quarter vs. the 7.9% expected at the end of September. A continued pickup in merger and acquisition activity was also a positive for market sentiment.

The Fed built on its September rate cut by delivering two additional 25 basis-point reductions at the final meetings of the year, with a weakening employment picture prompting the FOMC to move policy closer to neutral. December commentary was received as more balanced (less hawkish) than expected. Trade developments, recently a source of uncertainty, were positive on balance, with the U.S. and China agreeing to extend their trade truce for a year.

Potential concerns include a softening labor market, consumer cautiousness and affordability issues (largely impacting the lower end of the income spectrum thus far), housing market weakness, and lingering concerns over trade/tariffs and international relations. Concerns about future Fed independence remain in the background as well.

We continue to monitor employment, housing, manufacturing, and market breadth data, among others, to continually reassess our views. Consistent with the last few years, we maintain a high-quality Fund with a focus on higher return on investment businesses with pricing power. One hundred percent of the Fund (excluding cash) remains invested in companies we see as having moderate or high barriers to entry. We believe disciplined execution of our process will benefit the Fund through periods of volatility and over the long term.

data.title: Victory Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Executive Summary

The fourth quarter embodied market contradictions: GDP surged to 4.3%, while inflation remained stubbornly above the Federal Reserve's 2% target and unemployment was slightly elevated at 4.4%. Two 25-basis point rate cuts sparked multiple FOMC dissents, revealing deep divisions over mandate prioritization. The government shutdown disrupted critical data collection, obscuring an already murky economic picture. Fixed income markets mirrored this complexity with positive performance for the quarter and compressed credit spreads that signaled risk appetite despite continued uncertainty. Against this backdrop, the Victory Income Fund (Institutional Shares) underperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, for the quarter ended December 31, 2025.

Market Update & Commentary

Markets saw a continuation of contradictory themes in Q4. Inflation declined throughout the quarter, though it remained above the Fed's 2% target. Year-over-year, the Consumer Price Index (CPI) declined from 3.0% at the end of Q3 to 2.7%. Year-over-year Core CPI, which excludes food and energy, declined from 3.0% to 2.6%. The unemployment rate was unchanged quarter-over-quarter, concluding the year at 4.4%, though it did peak mid-quarter at 4.5%. Yet GDP defied the slowdown narrative entirely, with growth surging to 4.3% annualized quarter-over-quarter and surpassing forecasts. This economic picture became murkier mid-quarter when the longest government shutdown in history disrupted data collection, resulting in a missing CPI report and clouding investors' view of an already uncertain economy.

The Fed's two 25-bps cuts in Q4 – ostensibly responding to labor market softening – revealed deeper fault lines. Multiple dissents at both Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meetings highlighted internal division over whether inflation or employment should drive policy. This internal discord was compounded by external pressure to reduce rates from the executive branch. We saw investor sensitivity to perceived threats to central bank independence in Q4 and questions about institutional credibility that will likely intensify as Powell's time as chair of the FOMC comes to an end.

Fixed income markets embodied the quarter's contradictions: while returns were positive, the story beneath was complex. The Treasury curve steepened as short-term yields declined in response to the two reductions in the federal funds rate, yet long-term yields rose. This move reflected persistent investor concerns about fiscal spending and inflation. Meanwhile, corporate credit spreads remained meaningfully below long-term averages for both investment grade and high-yield-rated debt in Q4, signaling the same risk-on sentiment driving equities to record highs even as economic uncertainty mounted.

Portfolio Performance & Positioning

After increasing the allocation to corporates during the second quarter as spreads widened in response to tariff concerns, the Fund continued to decrease its allocation to corporate securities as spreads tightened back in throughout the rest of the year. In particular, the Fund reduced its allocation to BBB-rated corporate securities. With the proceeds from these sells, the Fund increased its allocation to asset-backed securities (ABS) and agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS). The Fund's duration remains in line with its benchmark.

Contributors

  • The Fund's overweight allocation to corporate bonds contributed positively to performance during the quarter.
  • Security selection within financials and utilities contributed most to performance.
  • Security selection within BBB-rated securities was the largest credit rating factor benefiting performance.

Detractors

  • An underweight allocation to agency MBS, which was the best-performing sector within the benchmark, was the largest detractor from performance.
  • Sectors that detracted from performance included consumer cyclicals and healthcare.
  • ABS also detracted from performance.
  • AA-rated securities, which includes agency MBS, were the largest detractor from performance.
